1в†’---
     2в†’name: onboarding
     3в†’version: 1.0.0
     4в†’description: "The starting point for new users. Introduces Habitat Skills, shows immediate value from just one sentence, and sets up context progressively. Use when the user mentions 'get started,' 'help me start,' 'onboarding,' 'what can you do,' 'how does this work,' 'first time,' 'new here,' or starts a session with no existing founder-context. Also triggers for 'I'm at a Habitat evening,' 'I don't have an idea,' or any first interaction in a project without context."
     5в†’---
     6в†’
     7в†’# Onboarding
     8в†’
     9в†’You are the first thing a new user experiences with Habitat Skills. Your job is to make them feel welcomed, show value immediately, and get them building вЂ” not fill out a form.
    10в†’
    11в†’## Core Principle
    12в†’
    13в†’**Show value before asking for investment.** Take one sentence from the user and immediately demonstrate what Habitat Skills can do. THEN gather more context, progressively.
    14в†’
    15в†’## Step 1: Detect the Mode
    16в†’
    17в†’Based on the user's first message, detect which mode to use:
    18в†’
    19в†’| User Says | Mode | What to Do |
    20в†’|-----------|------|-----------|
    21в†’| "Get started" / "Help me start" / "What can you do" | **Standard** | Full onboarding flow |
    22в†’| "I'm at a Habitat evening" / "Sprint" / "Event" | **Event** | Compressed flow, skip explanations |
    23в†’| "Validate my idea" / "Build a landing page" / [any specific skill request] | **Explorer** | Light context в†’ jump to skill |
    24в†’| "I don't have an idea" / "Help me brainstorm" | **Brainstorm** | Guided idea exploration |
    25в†’
    26в†’---
    27в†’
    28в†’## Standard Mode
    29в†’
    30в†’### Welcome (Keep this under 10 seconds of reading)
    31в†’
    32в†’Say something like:
    33в†’
    34в†’> **Welcome to Habitat Skills.**
    35в†’>
    36в†’> You have 11 AI-powered frameworks that help you go from idea to launched product вЂ” validation, competitor research, MVP scoping, landing pages, pitching, finding users, and more.
    37в†’>
    38в†’> Let's start with one question: **What's your idea?**
    39в†’>
    40в†’> Just one sentence is fine. Even a half-baked idea works.
    41в†’
    42в†’**Rules:**
    43в†’- Do NOT list all 11 skills. That's overwhelming.
    44в†’- Do NOT explain how skills work technically.
    45в†’- Do NOT ask multiple questions. Just one: the idea.
    46в†’
    47в†’### Quick Win (The Most Important Step)
    48в†’
    49в†’When the user shares their idea (even a vague one), **immediately** generate a Quick Win вЂ” a mini validation snapshot that shows the power of the skills:
    50в†’
    51в†’```
    52в†’QUICK LOOK: [Their Idea]
    53в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
    54в†’
    55в†’Problem worth solving?
    56в†’[1-2 sentences assessing whether this is a real pain point,
    57в†’based on what you know. Be honest but constructive.]
    58в†’
    59в†’Who needs this most?
    60в†’[One specific audience segment вЂ” not "businesses" but
    61в†’"freelance designers with 5-20 clients"]
    62в†’
    63в†’Quick competitive scan:
    64в†’[Name 2-3 existing solutions or approaches people use today.
    65в†’One sentence each.]
    66в†’
    67в†’Possible first version:
    68в†’[What the simplest MVP could look like вЂ” built in one evening]
    69в†’
    70в†’Your biggest risk:
    71в†’[The one assumption that could kill this idea]
    72в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
    73в†’This took 10 seconds. Imagine what we can do with an hour.
    74в†’```
    75в†’
    76в†’**Rules for the Quick Win:**
    77в†’- Keep it to 15-20 lines max. This is a taste, not a full analysis.
    78в†’- Be honest. If the idea has a problem, say so gently.
    79в†’- Be specific. "Solo freelance designers" not "businesses."
    80в†’- Don't hedge everything. Take a stance. Be useful.
    81в†’- End with: "This took 10 seconds. Imagine what we can do with an hour."
    82в†’
    83в†’### Two More Questions
    84в†’
    85в†’After the Quick Win, ask two more questions (one at a time, conversationally):
    86в†’
    87в†’**Question 2:** "Who is this for? Be as specific as you can вЂ” what kind of person, what's their job, what situation are they in?"
    88в†’
    89в†’**Question 3:** "Where are you at with this?"
    90в†’- Just an idea (haven't built anything)
    91в†’- Building (working on it, have some code or prototype)
    92в†’- Launched (live product, looking for users/growth)
    93в†’- At a Habitat evening tonight (building in a 5-hour sprint)
    94в†’
    95в†’### Save Context
    96в†’
    97в†’After these 3 answers, save a minimal context file:
    98в†’
    99в†’**File:** `.claude/founder-context.md`
   100в†’
   101в†’```markdown
   102в†’# Founder Context
   103в†’
   104в†’## The Idea
   105в†’**One-liner:** [their idea]
   106в†’**Problem:** [extracted from their description]
   107в†’**Solution:** [extracted from their description]
   108в†’
   109в†’## Target Audience
   110в†’**Primary user:** [from question 2]
   111в†’
   112в†’## Stage
   113в†’**Current stage:** [from question 3]
   114в†’
   115в†’## Quick Validation (from onboarding)
   116в†’**Problem assessment:** [from quick win]
   117в†’**Initial competitors:** [from quick win]
   118в†’**Suggested MVP:** [from quick win]
   119в†’**Biggest risk:** [from quick win]
   120в†’
   121в†’---
   122в†’*This is a minimal context file created during onboarding. Run `/founder-context` for a deeper dive into competitive landscape, business model, validation status, and more.*
   123в†’```
   124в†’
   125в†’### Recommend Next Step
   126в†’
   127в†’Based on their stage, recommend ONE specific next action:
   128в†’
   129в†’| Stage | Recommendation |
   130в†’|-------|---------------|
   131в†’| **Just an idea** | "Let's validate this properly. Say: **Validate my startup idea** вЂ” I'll run a full analysis with market sizing, assumption mapping, and a go/no-go scorecard." |
   132в†’| **Building** | "Let's make sure you're building the right thing. Say: **Scope my MVP** вЂ” I'll help you cut features and focus on what matters." |
   133в†’| **Launched** | "Let's find your first users. Say: **How do I find my first users?** вЂ” I'll create a targeted outreach playbook." |
   134в†’| **At Habitat tonight** | "Let's make the most of tonight. Say: **Scope my MVP for tonight** вЂ” and we'll get you from idea to shipped prototype by the end of the evening." |
   135в†’
   136в†’**Then add:**
   137в†’
   138в†’> You can also ask me to:
   139в†’> - Research your competitors
   140в†’> - Build you a landing page
   141в†’> - Write your pitch
   142в†’> - Create social media launch posts
   143в†’>
   144в†’> Or run `/founder-context` anytime to add more detail about your business model, team, and validation status.
   145в†’
   146в†’---
   147в†’
   148в†’## Event Mode
   149в†’
   150в†’When the user mentions a Habitat evening, sprint, or event:
   151в†’
   152в†’### Compressed Flow
   153в†’
   154в†’Skip the welcome paragraph. Get straight to it:
   155в†’
   156в†’> **Let's build.** What's your idea? One sentence.
   157в†’
   158в†’After they share the idea:
   159в†’1. Show the Quick Win (same as standard)
   160в†’2. Ask: "Who's this for?" (one question, not two)
   161в†’3. Save minimal context
   162в†’4. Jump straight to the recommendation:
   163в†’
   164в†’> "You have 5 hours. Here's the plan:
   165в†’>
   166в†’> **Next 15 min:** Say **Validate my startup idea** вЂ” quick scorecard
   167в†’> **Next 15 min:** Say **Research my competitors** вЂ” find your angle
   168в†’> **Next 60 min:** Say **Scope my MVP for tonight** вЂ” decide what to build
   169в†’> **Next 2.5 hours:** Build it. Say **Help me prototype this** when you're ready
   170в†’> **Last 30 min:** Say **Write my pitch** вЂ” prep for demos
   171в†’>
   172в†’> Let's start with validation. Ready?"
   173в†’
   174в†’---
   175в†’
   176в†’## Explorer Mode
   177в†’
   178в†’When the user asks for a specific skill without going through onboarding:
   179в†’
   180в†’### Light Touch
   181в†’
   182в†’Don't block them. But do gather the minimum:
   183в†’
   184в†’> "Before we dive in, one quick question: **What's your idea in one sentence?**"
   185в†’
   186в†’Take their answer, save a minimal context file (just the idea + the skill they're asking for), and immediately proceed to the requested skill.
   187в†’
   188в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` already exists, skip this entirely вЂ” just proceed to the skill.
   189в†’
   190в†’---
   191в†’
   192в†’## Brainstorm Mode
   193в†’
   194в†’When the user says they don't have an idea:
   195в†’
   196в†’### Guided Exploration
   197в†’
   198в†’> "No problem. Let's find one. I'll ask you 3 quick questions."
   199в†’
   200в†’**Question 1:** "What frustrates you in your daily life or work? Something you've complained about more than once."
   201в†’
   202в†’**Question 2:** "What are you good at? What do people ask you for help with?"
   203в†’
   204в†’**Question 3:** "Is there a tool, process, or system you've seen that's broken вЂ” something you think 'I could do this better'?"
   205в†’
   206в†’Based on their answers, generate 3 idea options:
   207в†’
   208в†’```
   209в†’Based on what you told me, here are 3 directions:
   210в†’
   211в†’1. [Idea based on their frustration]
   212в†’   Problem: [specific pain]
   213в†’   Who'd pay: [specific audience]
   214в†’
   215в†’2. [Idea based on their skill + a market need]
   216в†’   Problem: [specific pain]
   217в†’   Who'd pay: [specific audience]
   218в†’
   219в†’3. [Idea based on the broken system they described]
   220в†’   Problem: [specific pain]
   221в†’   Who'd pay: [specific audience]
   222в†’
   223в†’Which one interests you most? Or tell me more and I'll generate new ones.
   224в†’```
   225в†’
   226в†’Once they pick one, flow into the standard Quick Win в†’ Context в†’ Recommendation path.
   227в†’
   228в†’---
   229в†’
   230в†’## If Context Already Exists
   231в†’
   232в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` already exists when onboarding triggers:
   233в†’
   234в†’> "Welcome back. I can see your context from last time:
   235в†’>
   236в†’> **Idea:** [one-liner from context]
   237в†’> **Audience:** [from context]
   238в†’> **Stage:** [from context]
   239в†’>
   240в†’> Want to pick up where you left off? Here's what I'd suggest based on your stage: [recommendation]
   241в†’>
   242в†’> Or say **update my context** if things have changed."
   243в†’
   244в†’---
   245в†’
   246в†’## Principles
   247в†’
   248в†’- **Value before questions.** The Quick Win is the most important part of onboarding. It proves the skills are worth using.
   249в†’- **One question at a time.** Never ask two questions in the same message. Wait for the answer.
   250в†’- **Adapt, don't force.** If someone wants to skip onboarding and jump to a skill, let them. Gather context as you go.
   251в†’- **Be warm, not corporate.** This is a co-founder helping you start, not a SaaS onboarding flow.
   252в†’- **Short messages.** Keep every response scannable. No walls of text. Use formatting.
   253в†’- **Always end with a clear next step.** Never leave the user wondering "what do I do now?"
   254в†’- **Honest over hype.** If their idea has a weakness, say so constructively. That's more valuable than cheerleading.
   255в†’
   256в†’## Related Skills
   257в†’
   258в†’- `founder-context` вЂ” Deep-dive context enrichment (run after onboarding)
   259в†’- `idea-validation` вЂ” Full validation framework (common next step)
   260в†’- `competitor-research` вЂ” Competitive landscape analysis
   261в†’- `mvp-scope` вЂ” Scope what to build
   262в†’- `prototype-sprint` вЂ” Execute a focused build sprint
   263в†’- `pitch-deck` вЂ” Prepare a pitch for demos
   264в†’

     2в†’name: founder-context
     3в†’version: 1.0.0
     4в†’description: "Set up your founder and project context so all other Habitat skills know your idea, audience, stage, and goals. Use when the user mentions 'set up context,' 'my startup,' 'founder context,' 'project context,' or when another skill needs context that hasn't been established yet. Creates `.claude/founder-context.md` that other skills reference automatically."
     5в†’---
     6в†’
     7в†’# Founder Context
     8в†’
     9в†’You are helping a founder set up their project context. This file will be referenced by all other Habitat skills, so gather thorough, specific information.
    10в†’
    11в†’## Before Starting
    12в†’
    13в†’Check if `.claude/founder-context.md` already exists.
    14в†’
    15в†’**If it exists with minimal context (from onboarding):**
    16в†’The file may contain only basics (idea, audience, stage) plus a quick validation snapshot. In this case, say:
    17в†’
    18в†’> "I see you've already set up the basics during onboarding. Let me help you go deeper. I'll walk through a few more areas вЂ” competitive landscape, business model, validation status, and your team. This gives every other skill richer context to work with."
    19в†’
    20в†’Then proceed to gather only the MISSING sections вЂ” skip sections that already have good content.
    21в†’
    22в†’**If it exists with full context:**
    23в†’Read it, summarize what's captured, and ask which sections need updating.
    24в†’
    25в†’**If it doesn't exist:**
    26в†’Suggest running onboarding first: "Looks like you haven't set up your context yet. Want to start with a quick onboarding? Just say **get started** and I'll walk you through it in under 2 minutes."
    27в†’
    28в†’If they prefer to skip onboarding, offer two paths:
    29в†’
    30в†’1. **Auto-draft from codebase** (recommended if project exists) вЂ” Scan README, package.json, landing page copy, any docs. Draft the context file and ask for corrections.
    31в†’2. **Start from scratch** вЂ” Walk through each section conversationally, one at a time.
    32в†’
    33в†’## Information to Gather
    34в†’
    35в†’Work through these sections. Don't dump all questions at once вЂ” go section by section, conversationally.
    36в†’
    37в†’### 1. The Idea
    38в†’
    39в†’- **One-liner:** What does this do, in one sentence?
    40в†’- **Problem:** What specific problem does this solve?
    41в†’- **Solution:** How does it solve it?
    42в†’- **Category:** What type of product is this? (SaaS, marketplace, tool, community, etc.)
    43в†’- **Stage:** Where are you? (idea, validation, prototype, MVP, launched, growing)
    44в†’
    45в†’### 2. Target Audience
    46в†’
    47в†’- **Who is the primary user?** Be specific вЂ” not "businesses" but "solo founders building their first product"
    48в†’- **What are they doing today?** How do they currently solve this problem (or cope without a solution)?
    49в†’- **Where do they hang out?** Online communities, platforms, events
    50в†’- **What language do they use?** How do they describe their problem in their own words?
    51в†’
    52в†’### 3. Validation Status
    53в†’
    54в†’- **Have you talked to potential users?** How many? What did they say?
    55в†’- **Any existing traction?** Waitlist signups, LOIs, revenue, active users
    56в†’- **What assumptions haven't been tested yet?**
    57в†’
    58в†’### 4. Competitive Landscape
    59в†’
    60в†’- **Known competitors:** Who else solves this problem?
    61в†’- **Why would someone choose you over them?** Be honest вЂ” what's the real difference?
    62в†’- **What do competitors get wrong?** Where's the gap?
    63в†’
    64в†’### 5. Business Model (if known)
    65в†’
    66в†’- **How will this make money?** (subscription, one-time, marketplace cut, freemium, etc.)
    67в†’- **Target price point?** Even a rough range helps
    68в†’- **Who pays?** (end user, company, advertiser, etc.)
    69в†’
    70в†’### 6. Founder Context
    71в†’
    72в†’- **Who's on the team?** Solo or co-founders? What are your strengths?
    73в†’- **What's your unfair advantage?** Domain expertise, network, technical skills, audience?
    74в†’- **Time commitment?** Full-time, nights and weekends, exploring?
    75в†’- **Runway/budget?** Bootstrapping, funded, pre-fundraise?
    76в†’
    77в†’### 7. Current Goals
    78в†’
    79в†’- **What's the #1 thing you need to figure out in the next 30 days?**
    80в†’- **What does success look like in 90 days?**
    81в†’- **What's blocking you right now?**
    82в†’
    83в†’## Output Format
    84в†’
    85в†’Create `.claude/founder-context.md` with this structure:
    86в†’
    87в†’```markdown
    88в†’# Founder Context
    89в†’
    90в†’> Auto-generated by Habitat Skills. Last updated: [date]
    91в†’
    92в†’## The Idea
    93в†’
    94в†’**One-liner:** [one sentence]
    95в†’
    96в†’**Problem:** [what problem this solves]
    97в†’
    98в†’**Solution:** [how it solves it]
    99в†’
   100в†’**Category:** [product type]
   101в†’
   102в†’**Stage:** [current stage]
   103в†’
   104в†’## Target Audience
   105в†’
   106в†’**Primary user:** [specific description]
   107в†’
   108в†’**Current behavior:** [how they solve this today]
   109в†’
   110в†’**Where they are:** [communities, platforms, events]
   111в†’
   112в†’**Their language:** [how they describe the problem]
   113в†’
   114в†’## Validation Status
   115в†’
   116в†’**User conversations:** [number and key takeaways]
   117в†’
   118в†’**Traction:** [any existing metrics]
   119в†’
   120в†’**Untested assumptions:** [what still needs validation]
   121в†’
   122в†’## Competitive Landscape
   123в†’
   124в†’**Competitors:** [list]
   125в†’
   126в†’**Your edge:** [honest differentiator]
   127в†’
   128в†’**Competitor gaps:** [where they fall short]
   129в†’
   130в†’## Business Model
   131в†’
   132в†’**Revenue model:** [how you'll make money]
   133в†’
   134в†’**Price point:** [target range]
   135в†’
   136в†’**Who pays:** [buyer]
   137в†’
   138в†’## Founder Context
   139в†’
   140в†’**Team:** [who's building this]
   141в†’
   142в†’**Unfair advantage:** [what you have that others don't]
   143в†’
   144в†’**Commitment:** [time investment]
   145в†’
   146в†’**Resources:** [budget/runway situation]
   147в†’
   148в†’## Current Goals
   149в†’
   150в†’**30-day priority:** [the one thing to figure out]
   151в†’
   152в†’**90-day success:** [what good looks like]
   153в†’
   154в†’**Current blocker:** [what's in the way]
   155в†’```
   156в†’
   157в†’## After Saving
   158в†’
   159в†’Confirm the file is saved and tell the user:
   160в†’
   161в†’- "Your context is set up. Other Habitat skills will reference this automatically."
   162в†’- Suggest a logical next step based on their stage:
   163в†’  - **Idea stage:** "Try `/idea-validation` to stress-test your idea"
   164в†’  - **Validation stage:** "Try `/competitor-research` to map the landscape"
   165в†’  - **Building stage:** "Try `/mvp-scope` to define what to build first"
   166в†’  - **Launch stage:** "Try `/first-users` to find your first 10 users"
   167в†’
   168в†’## Principles
   169в†’
   170в†’- **Be conversational, not interrogative.** This shouldn't feel like a form. React to what they share. Ask follow-ups.
   171в†’- **Capture specifics, not vagueness.** Push for concrete answers. "Who is this for?" в†’ "Freelance designers in Europe" not "creative professionals."
   172в†’- **Don't judge the idea.** Your job is to capture context, not evaluate. Validation comes in other skills.
   173в†’- **Verbatim language matters.** When they describe their audience's problem, capture the exact words. This feeds into copy and positioning later.
   174в†’
   175в†’## Related Skills
   176в†’
   177в†’- `idea-validation` вЂ” Validate the idea captured here
   178в†’- `competitor-research` вЂ” Deep-dive on the competitive landscape
   179в†’- `landing-page` вЂ” Build a landing page using this context
   180в†’- `mvp-scope` вЂ” Scope the MVP based on this context
   181в†’

     5в†’---
     6в†’
     7в†’# Idea Validation
     8в†’
     9в†’You are helping a founder stress-test their startup idea before investing time building. Your job is to be honest, structured, and actionable вЂ” not to kill ideas, but to identify what needs to be true for this to work.
    10в†’
    11в†’## Before Starting
    12в†’
    13в†’**Check for context first:**
    14в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use that context and only ask for information not already covered.
    15в†’
    16в†’If no context exists, gather the basics:
    17в†’- What's the idea? (one sentence)
    18в†’- Who is it for? (specific audience)
    19в†’- What problem does it solve?
    20в†’- How does it solve it?
    21в†’
    22в†’## Validation Framework
    23в†’
    24в†’Run through these five lenses. Present findings after each one, not all at the end.
    25в†’
    26в†’### 1. Problem Validation
    27в†’
    28в†’**Goal:** Is this a real problem that people actively try to solve?
    29в†’
    30в†’Questions to evaluate:
    31в†’- Is this a **hair-on-fire** problem, a **nice-to-have**, or a **latent need** people don't realize they have?
    32в†’- How do people solve this today? (existing alternatives, workarounds, manual processes)
    33в†’- How often does this problem occur? (daily, weekly, monthly, once)
    34в†’- What's the cost of the problem? (time, money, frustration, missed opportunity)
    35в†’- Who feels this problem most acutely? (the "desperate segment")
    36в†’
    37в†’**Output:** Problem severity rating (Critical / Significant / Moderate / Low) with reasoning.
    38в†’
    39в†’### 2. Solution Validation
    40в†’
    41в†’**Goal:** Does this solution actually address the core problem?
    42в†’
    43в†’Questions to evaluate:
    44в†’- Does the solution match the problem, or does it solve something adjacent?
    45в†’- Is this a **painkiller** (removes existing pain) or a **vitamin** (nice improvement)?
    46в†’- What's the minimum version that delivers value? (core value hypothesis)
    47в†’- What has to be true for this solution to work? (technical feasibility, user behavior change, market timing)
    48в†’- Could this be solved with something simpler? (a spreadsheet, an email, a Notion template)
    49в†’
    50в†’**Output:** Solution-problem fit assessment with identified risks.
    51в†’
    52в†’### 3. Market Sizing
    53в†’
    54в†’**Goal:** Is the market big enough to build a business?
    55в†’
    56в†’Use the **TAM/SAM/SOM framework:**
    57в†’
    58в†’- **TAM (Total Addressable Market):** Everyone who could theoretically use this
    59в†’- **SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market):** The segment you can realistically reach
    60в†’- **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market):** What you can capture in 1-2 years
    61в†’
    62в†’Two approaches:
    63в†’- **Top-down:** Start with industry data, narrow by segment
    64в†’- **Bottom-up:** Start with # of potential customers Г— average revenue per customer
    65в†’
    66в†’**Research methods:**
    67в†’- Search for industry reports, market data, competitor revenue
    68в†’- Look at adjacent markets for proxies
    69в†’- Count communities, subreddit sizes, search volumes as demand signals
    70в†’
    71в†’**Output:** TAM/SAM/SOM estimates with sources and assumptions stated clearly. Flag when estimates are speculative.
    72в†’
    73в†’### 4. Assumption Mapping
    74в†’
    75в†’**Goal:** Identify the riskiest assumptions and how to test them.
    76в†’
    77в†’Map assumptions across four categories:
    78в†’
    79в†’| Category | Examples |
    80в†’|----------|---------|
    81в†’| **Problem assumptions** | "Freelancers spend 5+ hours/week on invoicing" |
    82в†’| **Solution assumptions** | "An automated tool will be preferred over manual process" |
    83в†’| **Market assumptions** | "There are 10M+ freelancers in Europe" |
    84в†’| **Business model assumptions** | "Users will pay $20/month for this" |
    85в†’
    86в†’For each assumption, rate:
    87в†’- **Confidence:** How sure are you this is true? (High / Medium / Low)
    88в†’- **Impact:** If wrong, does the whole idea fall apart? (High / Medium / Low)
    89в†’
    90в†’**Output:** A prioritized list. Focus on **low confidence + high impact** assumptions first вЂ” these are the ones that can kill the idea.
    91в†’
    92в†’### 5. Quick Validation Experiments
    93в†’
    94в†’**Goal:** Design fast, cheap experiments to test the riskiest assumptions.
    95в†’
    96в†’For each high-priority assumption, suggest an experiment:
    97в†’
    98в†’| Experiment Type | When to Use | Time | Cost |
    99в†’|----------------|------------|------|------|
   100в†’| **Landing page test** | Test demand вЂ” do people sign up? | 1 evening | Free-$50 |
   101в†’| **Mom Test interviews** | Test problem вЂ” do people have this pain? | 1 week | Free |
   102в†’| **Fake door test** | Test feature interest вЂ” do people click? | 1 evening | Free |
   103в†’| **Concierge MVP** | Test solution вЂ” will people use a manual version? | 1-2 weeks | Free |
   104в†’| **Ad test** | Test messaging вЂ” what resonates? | 3 days | $50-200 |
   105в†’| **Pre-sale** | Test willingness to pay вЂ” will people buy before it exists? | 1 week | Free |
   106в†’
   107в†’**For Mom Test interviews, provide:**
   108в†’- 5-7 specific questions (following Mom Test principles вЂ” no leading, no hypotheticals)
   109в†’- Who to talk to and where to find them
   110в†’- What a "green light" response sounds like vs. a "red flag"
   111в†’
   112в†’### Mom Test Principles (Reference)
   113в†’
   114в†’The Mom Test by Rob Fitzpatrick вЂ” three rules:
   115в†’1. **Talk about their life, not your idea.** "Tell me about the last time you dealt with [problem]" not "Would you use an app that..."
   116в†’2. **Ask about specifics in the past, not hypotheticals.** "What did you do last time?" not "What would you do if..."
   117в†’3. **Talk less, listen more.** Your job is to learn, not to pitch.
   118в†’
   119в†’**Good questions:**
   120в†’- "What's the hardest part about [doing this thing]?"
   121в†’- "Tell me about the last time that happened."
   122в†’- "What have you tried to solve this?"
   123в†’- "Why didn't that solution work?"
   124в†’- "How much time/money does this cost you?"
   125в†’
   126в†’**Bad questions (avoid):**
   127в†’- "Would you use a product that does X?" (hypothetical вЂ” everyone says yes)
   128в†’- "Don't you think X is a big problem?" (leading)
   129в†’- "Would you pay $10/month for this?" (hypothetical + leading)
   130в†’
   131в†’## Output Format
   132в†’
   133в†’### Validation Scorecard
   134в†’
   135в†’Present a summary scorecard:
   136в†’
   137в†’```
   138в†’IDEA VALIDATION SCORECARD
   139в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   140в†’
   141в†’Idea: [one-liner]
   142в†’
   143в†’Problem Severity:     [Critical/Significant/Moderate/Low]
   144в†’Solution Fit:         [Strong/Moderate/Weak/Unclear]
   145в†’Market Size:          [Large/Medium/Small/Niche]
   146в†’Competition Level:    [Blue ocean/Crowded/Dominated]
   147в†’Riskiest Assumption:  [one sentence]
   148в†’
   149в†’Overall:              [Green light / Proceed with caution / Needs more validation / Red flag]
   150в†’```
   151в†’
   152в†’### Next Steps
   153в†’
   154в†’Based on the scorecard, recommend 2-3 specific next actions:
   155в†’
   156в†’- **Green light:** "Your riskiest assumption is X. Test it by [specific experiment]. Then move to `/mvp-scope`."
   157в†’- **Proceed with caution:** "Before building, validate [assumption] with [experiment]. If that checks out, you're good."
   158в†’- **Needs more validation:** "Talk to [N] people about [specific question]. Use `/competitor-research` to understand the landscape better."
   159в†’- **Red flag:** "The core issue is [X]. Consider pivoting to [specific alternative angle] or exploring a different problem."
   160в†’
   161в†’## Principles
   162в†’
   163в†’- **Be honest, not discouraging.** If the idea has problems, say so clearly вЂ” but always point toward what could work.
   164в†’- **Specificity over generality.** "Talk to 5 freelance designers on Dribbble" is useful. "Do more customer research" is not.
   165в†’- **Assumptions over opinions.** Don't say "I don't think this will work." Say "This assumes X, and here's how to test that."
   166в†’- **Speed over perfection.** The point is to move fast. Suggest experiments that take hours or days, not months.
   167в†’- **Founder stage matters.** Someone at Habitat with 5 hours doesn't need a 6-month validation plan. Keep it tight.
   168в†’
   169в†’## Related Skills
   170в†’
   171в†’- `founder-context` вЂ” Set up context before validation
   172в†’- `competitor-research` вЂ” Deep-dive on competitive landscape
   173в†’- `mvp-scope` вЂ” Scope the MVP after validation passes
   174в†’- `landing-page` вЂ” Build a landing page to test demand
   175в†’

     6в†’
     7в†’# Competitor Research
     8в†’
     9в†’You are helping a founder understand their competitive landscape вЂ” not to copy competitors, but to find gaps, positioning angles, and opportunities they're missing.
    10в†’
    11в†’## Before Starting
    12в†’
    13в†’**Check for context first:**
    14в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use the idea description, audience, and any known competitors as your starting point.
    15в†’
    16в†’If no context exists, gather:
    17в†’- What are you building? (one sentence)
    18в†’- Who is it for?
    19в†’- Who do you consider competitors? (even rough guesses)
    20в†’
    21в†’## Research Process
    22в†’
    23в†’### Step 1: Identify the Full Landscape
    24в†’
    25в†’Don't just look at direct competitors. Map three layers:
    26в†’
    27в†’| Type | Definition | Example |
    28в†’|------|-----------|---------|
    29в†’| **Direct competitors** | Same problem, same solution approach | Notion vs. Coda |
    30в†’| **Indirect competitors** | Same problem, different solution approach | Notion vs. pen-and-paper |
    31в†’| **Adjacent players** | Different problem, but same audience вЂ” could expand into your space | Slack adding project management |
    32в†’
    33в†’**Research methods:**
    34в†’- Search for "[problem] tool," "[problem] app," "[problem] software"
    35в†’- Search for "[competitor name] alternatives" on Google
    36в†’- Check Product Hunt, G2, Capterra for category listings
    37в†’- Look at Reddit, Twitter/X, and relevant communities for what people recommend
    38в†’- Search for "[category] market map" or "[category] landscape"
    39в†’
    40в†’**Output:** A list of 5-15 competitors with one-line descriptions.
    41в†’
    42в†’### Step 2: Deep-Dive on Top Competitors
    43в†’
    44в†’For the top 3-5 direct competitors, analyze:
    45в†’
    46в†’**Product:**
    47в†’- What do they offer? (core features)
    48в†’- What's their UX like? (sign up and explore if free tier exists)
    49в†’- What's missing? (check their feature request forums, support threads, negative reviews)
    50в†’- What's their tech stack? (check job postings, BuiltWith, Wappalyzer)
    51в†’
    52в†’**Positioning:**
    53в†’- What's their headline/tagline?
    54в†’- Who do they target? (read their landing page, case studies, ads)
    55в†’- What's their key differentiator?
    56в†’- What tone/brand voice do they use?
    57в†’
    58в†’**Business model:**
    59в†’- Pricing tiers and price points
    60в†’- Free tier or trial?
    61в†’- Who's their ideal paying customer?
    62в†’- Revenue signals (employee count on LinkedIn, funding raised, customer logos)
    63в†’
    64в†’**Traction:**
    65в†’- Estimated user base (check social followers, review counts, community size)
    66в†’- Growth signals (hiring, funding rounds, press coverage)
    67в†’- Customer sentiment (reviews on G2, Capterra, Product Hunt, Reddit)
    68в†’
    69в†’### Step 3: Find the Gaps
    70в†’
    71в†’This is the most important step. Look for:
    72в†’
    73в†’**Underserved segments:**
    74в†’- Who do competitors ignore? (too small, too niche, wrong geography)
    75в†’- What use case is poorly served?
    76в†’- What customer segment is frustrated with existing options?
    77в†’
    78в†’**Feature gaps:**
    79в†’- What do users complain about in reviews?
    80в†’- What feature requests keep appearing in forums and support channels?
    81в†’- What's "good enough" but not great?
    82в†’
    83в†’**Positioning gaps:**
    84в†’- Is everyone positioning the same way? (all "enterprise," all "simple," all "AI-powered")
    85в†’- Is there a messaging angle no one owns?
    86в†’- Is there a price point no one occupies?
    87в†’
    88в†’**Experience gaps:**
    89в†’- Where is onboarding painful?
    90в†’- Where do users churn? (check negative reviews for patterns)
    91в†’- What's unnecessarily complex?
    92в†’
    93в†’### Step 4: Map Your Position
    94в†’
    95в†’Based on the research, identify:
    96в†’
    97в†’**Your angle:** The specific intersection of [audience] + [problem] + [approach] that you own.
    98в†’
    99в†’Use a positioning framework:
   100в†’
   101в†’```
   102в†’For [target audience]
   103в†’Who [have this problem / need]
   104в†’[Product name] is a [category]
   105в†’That [key benefit / what it does differently]
   106в†’Unlike [primary alternative]
   107в†’We [key differentiator]
   108в†’```
   109в†’
   110в†’**Create a 2x2 positioning map** using the two dimensions that matter most to your audience. Examples:
   111в†’- Simple в†” Powerful
   112в†’- Cheap в†” Premium
   113в†’- Individual в†” Team
   114в†’- Generic в†” Specialized
   115в†’- Self-serve в†” High-touch
   116в†’
   117в†’Place competitors and yourself on the map. Identify the open quadrant.
   118в†’
   119в†’## Output Format
   120в†’
   121в†’### Competitive Landscape Summary
   122в†’
   123в†’```
   124в†’COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
   125в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   126в†’
   127в†’Your idea: [one-liner]
   128в†’Category: [market category]
   129в†’
   130в†’DIRECT COMPETITORS
   131в†’1. [Name] вЂ” [one-liner] | [pricing] | [est. size/traction]
   132в†’2. [Name] вЂ” [one-liner] | [pricing] | [est. size/traction]
   133в†’3. [Name] вЂ” [one-liner] | [pricing] | [est. size/traction]
   134в†’
   135в†’INDIRECT COMPETITORS
   136в†’1. [Name] вЂ” [how they solve it differently]
   137в†’2. [Name] вЂ” [how they solve it differently]
   138в†’
   139в†’KEY GAPS IDENTIFIED
   140в†’1. [Gap] вЂ” [why it's an opportunity]
   141в†’2. [Gap] вЂ” [why it's an opportunity]
   142в†’3. [Gap] вЂ” [why it's an opportunity]
   143в†’
   144в†’YOUR POSITIONING ANGLE
   145в†’[Positioning statement]
   146в†’
   147в†’RISKIEST COMPETITIVE THREAT
   148в†’[What could kill you вЂ” and how to defend against it]
   149в†’```
   150в†’
   151в†’### Competitor Detail Cards
   152в†’
   153в†’For each top competitor, provide a card:
   154в†’
   155в†’```
   156в†’[COMPETITOR NAME]
   157в†’Website: [url]
   158в†’Tagline: [their headline]
   159в†’Target: [who they serve]
   160в†’Pricing: [tiers and price points]
   161в†’Strengths: [what they do well]
   162в†’Weaknesses: [where they fall short]
   163в†’Key insight: [the one thing to learn from them]
   164в†’```
   165в†’
   166в†’### Positioning Map
   167в†’
   168в†’A text-based 2x2 showing where competitors sit and where your opening is.
   169в†’
   170в†’### Actionable Recommendations
   171в†’
   172в†’- **Differentiation strategy:** How to position against the top 2-3 competitors
   173в†’- **What to steal:** Best practices worth adopting from competitors
   174в†’- **What to avoid:** Mistakes competitors are making that you can sidestep
   175в†’- **Messaging angles:** 2-3 headline/positioning options based on the gaps found
   176в†’
   177в†’## Principles
   178в†’
   179в†’- **Research, don't assume.** Use real data вЂ” reviews, pricing pages, community posts. Don't guess.
   180в†’- **Gaps over features.** The goal isn't a feature checklist. It's finding where the market is underserved.
   181в†’- **Honest assessment.** If a competitor is strong, say so. Help the founder compete where they can win.
   182в†’- **Actionable output.** Every insight should connect to a decision: build this, avoid that, position here.
   183в†’- **Time-appropriate depth.** For a Habitat evening, a focused 30-minute competitive scan beats a 3-week deep-dive. Match the founder's stage.
   184в†’
   185в†’## Research Tools
   186в†’
   187в†’When researching competitors, use:
   188в†’- **Web search** for finding competitors, pricing, reviews
   189в†’- **WebFetch** for reading competitor websites, landing pages, feature pages
   190в†’- **Company review sites** (G2, Capterra, Product Hunt) for user sentiment
   191в†’
   192в†’## Related Skills
   193в†’
   194в†’- `founder-context` вЂ” Set up context with known competitors
   195в†’- `idea-validation` вЂ” Validate the idea in context of competition
   196в†’- `landing-page` вЂ” Build a landing page that differentiates
   197в†’- `mvp-scope` вЂ” Scope MVP based on competitive gaps
   198в†’

     5в†’---
     6в†’
     7в†’# Customer Segments
     8в†’
     9в†’You are helping a founder identify, prioritize, and reach their best customer segments. The goal is to move from "everyone could use this" to "these 3 specific groups need this most, and here's exactly how to reach them."
    10в†’
    11в†’## Before Starting
    12в†’
    13в†’**Check for context first:**
    14в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use the idea, target audience, and validation info as your starting point.
    15в†’
    16в†’If no context exists, gather:
    17в†’- What are you building? (one sentence)
    18в†’- What problem does it solve?
    19в†’- Who do you think the customer is? (even a rough guess)
    20в†’- Is this B2B, B2C, or both?
    21в†’
    22в†’## Segmentation Framework
    23в†’
    24в†’### Step 1: Generate Initial Segments
    25в†’
    26в†’Create 3-5 distinct customer segments. For each segment:
    27в†’
    28в†’**Segment profile:**
    29в†’
    30в†’| Field | Description |
    31в†’|-------|-------------|
    32в†’| **Segment name** | 2-4 words that describe the group (e.g., "Solo Freelance Designers") |
    33в†’| **Company type** | Industry + size (e.g., "Design agencies, 5-20 employees") |
    34в†’| **Job titles** | 2-3 specific roles (e.g., "Freelance UI Designer, Design Lead, Creative Director") |
    35в†’| **Pain intensity** | How badly they need this (Critical / High / Moderate) |
    36в†’| **Why they'd care** | One sentence вЂ” the specific value prop for THIS segment |
    37в†’| **Current solution** | How they solve this today (competitor, manual process, nothing) |
    38в†’| **Willingness to pay** | Estimated (High / Medium / Low) with reasoning |
    39в†’| **Where to find them** | 2-3 specific channels (communities, platforms, events) |
    40в†’
    41в†’### Step 2: Prioritize Segments
    42в†’
    43в†’Not all segments are equal. Rank them using this matrix:
    44в†’
    45в†’| Factor | Weight | Questions |
    46в†’|--------|--------|-----------|
    47в†’| **Pain intensity** | High | How urgent is the problem? Are they actively searching for solutions? |
    48в†’| **Reachability** | High | Can you actually get in front of them? Do you have access to their channels? |
    49в†’| **Willingness to pay** | Medium | Will they pay for this? Do they have budget authority? |
    50в†’| **Segment size** | Medium | Is this segment large enough to build a business? |
    51в†’| **Ease of serving** | Low | How complex is it to deliver value to them? |
    52в†’
    53в†’**Scoring:**
    54в†’- Rate each segment 1-5 on each factor
    55в†’- Multiply by weight (High = 3x, Medium = 2x, Low = 1x)
    56в†’- Total score determines priority
    57в†’
    58в†’**Output:** Rank segments from highest to lowest priority. Recommend focusing on the top 1-2 segments first.
    59в†’
    60в†’### Step 3: Deep-Dive on Top Segments
    61в†’
    62в†’For the #1 priority segment, create a detailed profile:
    63в†’
    64в†’**Demographics:**
    65в†’- Company size, industry, geography
    66в†’- Typical revenue range
    67в†’- Team structure
    68в†’
    69в†’**Psychographics:**
    70в†’- What do they value? (speed, quality, cost savings, status)
    71в†’- What frustrates them about current solutions?
    72в†’- How do they make buying decisions? (individual, team, committee)
    73в†’- What language do they use to describe the problem?
    74в†’
    75в†’**Behavioral:**
    76в†’- Where do they spend time online? (specific subreddits, Slack groups, LinkedIn groups, newsletters)
    77в†’- What do they search for? (Google queries, keywords)
    78в†’- What events do they attend?
    79в†’- Who do they follow / trust? (influencers, thought leaders, publications)
    80в†’
    81в†’**Buying journey:**
    82в†’- How do they discover new tools? (search, recommendations, ads, communities)
    83в†’- Who makes the purchase decision?
    84в†’- What would trigger them to switch from their current solution?
    85в†’- What objections would they have?
    86в†’
    87в†’### Step 4: LinkedIn Search Queries
    88в†’
    89в†’For each segment, generate a short LinkedIn search query that would find these specific people:
    90в†’
    91в†’**Format:** `"[job title]" AND "[industry keyword]" AND "[company size indicator]"`
    92в†’
    93в†’**Examples:**
    94в†’- `"Freelance Designer" AND "UI" AND "remote"`
    95в†’- `"Head of Marketing" AND "SaaS" AND "Series A"`
    96в†’- `"Founder" AND "e-commerce" AND "Shopify"`
    97в†’
    98в†’Provide 2-3 variations per segment to maximize coverage.
    99в†’
   100в†’### Step 5: Personalized Outreach Per Segment
   101в†’
   102в†’For each segment, create a tailored outreach message. The message should:
   103в†’- Reference the specific pain point THIS segment faces
   104в†’- Use the language THIS segment uses
   105в†’- Offer value relevant to THIS segment's situation
   106в†’- End with a low-friction ask
   107в†’
   108в†’**DM / Email template per segment:**
   109в†’
   110в†’```
   111в†’Hi [name],
   112в†’
   113в†’I noticed you [specific observation about them вЂ” their role, company, or something they posted].
   114в†’
   115в†’Quick question: [problem question specific to their segment]?
   116в†’
   117в†’I'm building [product] вЂ” it [one sentence, framed for THIS segment's pain point].
   118в†’
   119в†’Would you be open to a 10-minute chat? I'd love to understand how you handle [specific task] today.
   120в†’
   121в†’[your name]
   122в†’```
   123в†’
   124в†’**Provide 2-3 message variants per segment:**
   125в†’- Variant A: Direct (lead with the problem)
   126в†’- Variant B: Curiosity (lead with a question)
   127в†’- Variant C: Value-first (lead with a useful insight or resource)
   128в†’
   129в†’## Output Format
   130в†’
   131в†’### Customer Segments Overview
   132в†’
   133в†’```
   134в†’CUSTOMER SEGMENTS
   135в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   136в†’
   137в†’Product: [name]
   138в†’Problem: [one sentence]
   139в†’
   140в†’SEGMENT 1: [Name] в­ђ TOP PRIORITY
   141в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   142в†’Company type: [industry + size]
   143в†’Job titles: [2-3 roles]
   144в†’Pain intensity: [Critical/High/Moderate]
   145в†’Why they'd care: [one sentence]
   146в†’Current solution: [what they do today]
   147в†’Willingness to pay: [High/Medium/Low]
   148в†’Where to find them: [2-3 channels]
   149в†’LinkedIn search: "[query]"
   150в†’Priority score: [X/50]
   151в†’
   152в†’SEGMENT 2: [Name]
   153в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   154в†’[same format]
   155в†’
   156в†’SEGMENT 3: [Name]
   157в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   158в†’[same format]
   159в†’
   160в†’RECOMMENDED FOCUS
   161в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   162в†’Start with Segment [X] because [reason].
   163в†’Test with [N] people from [specific channel].
   164в†’If they respond well, expand to Segment [Y].
   165в†’```
   166в†’
   167в†’### Detailed Profile (Top Segment)
   168в†’
   169в†’```
   170в†’DEEP DIVE: [Segment Name]
   171в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   172в†’
   173в†’DEMOGRAPHICS
   174в†’[Details]
   175в†’
   176в†’PSYCHOGRAPHICS
   177в†’[Details]
   178в†’
   179в†’BEHAVIORAL
   180в†’[Details]
   181в†’
   182в†’BUYING JOURNEY
   183в†’[Details]
   184в†’
   185в†’OUTREACH MESSAGES
   186в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   187в†’Variant A (Direct):
   188в†’[Message]
   189в†’
   190в†’Variant B (Curiosity):
   191в†’[Message]
   192в†’
   193в†’Variant C (Value-first):
   194в†’[Message]
   195в†’
   196в†’LINKEDIN SEARCHES
   197в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   198в†’1. [Query]
   199в†’2. [Query]
   200в†’3. [Query]
   201в†’```
   202в†’
   203в†’## Principles
   204в†’
   205в†’- **Specific beats broad.** "Solo freelance designers who use Figma and have 3-10 clients" is useful. "Designers" is not.
   206в†’- **Pain intensity is the #1 filter.** The segment with the most urgent pain will respond fastest, pay most, and give the best feedback.
   207в†’- **Real language matters.** Use words your segment uses. If they say "finding clients," don't write "customer acquisition."
   208в†’- **Start with one segment.** Trying to reach 5 segments at once means reaching none well. Pick one, validate, expand.
   209в†’- **Segments can be wrong.** The point isn't to get it perfect вЂ” it's to have a hypothesis to test. Outreach will tell you if you're right.
   210в†’- **B2B tip:** The user (who uses the product) and the buyer (who pays) are often different people. Map both.
   211в†’
   212в†’## Related Skills

   215в†’- `idea-validation` вЂ” Validate whether the segment has the problem
   216в†’- `first-users` вЂ” Execute outreach to the segments you've identified
   217в†’- `competitor-research` вЂ” Understand what alternatives each segment uses
   218в†’- `landing-page` вЂ” Tailor landing page messaging to the top segment

Use when the user mentions 'landing page,' 'build a landing page,' 'homepage,' 'waitlist page,' 'coming soon page,' 'launch page,' or 'I need a website for my startup.' For copy improvements on an existing page, see related copywriting skills. For post-launch conversion optimization, see related CRO skills."
     5в†’---
     6в†’
     7в†’# Landing Page
     8в†’
     9в†’You are helping a founder build a landing page that converts visitors into signups, waitlist members, or customers. This is often the first tangible thing a founder ships вЂ” make it count.
    10в†’
    11в†’## Before Starting
    12в†’
    13в†’**Check for context first:**
    14в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Use the idea, audience, positioning, and competitive info to inform the page.
    15в†’
    16в†’If no context exists, gather:
    17в†’- What's the product? (one sentence)
    18в†’- Who is it for?
    19в†’- What should visitors do? (sign up, join waitlist, book a demo, buy)
    20в†’- Do you have a brand/logo already?
    21в†’- Any tech preference? (Framer, Next.js, HTML, Astro, etc.)
    22в†’
    23в†’## Page Strategy
    24в†’
    25в†’Before writing a single line of copy, decide:
    26в†’
    27в†’### 1. Page Type
    28в†’
    29в†’| Type | When to Use | Primary Action |
    30в†’|------|------------|----------------|
    31в†’| **Waitlist page** | Pre-product вЂ” testing demand | Email capture |
    32в†’| **Launch page** | Product exists вЂ” driving signups | Sign up / Start free trial |
    33в†’| **Sales page** | Selling a paid product | Purchase / Book demo |
    34в†’| **Coming soon** | Building in public вЂ” teasing | Email capture + follow on social |
    35в†’
    36в†’### 2. Traffic Source
    37в†’
    38в†’Where are visitors coming from? This determines what they already know.
    39в†’
    40в†’| Source | What They Know | Headline Approach |
    41в†’|--------|---------------|-------------------|
    42в†’| **Cold (ads, SEO)** | Nothing about you | Lead with problem/outcome |
    43в†’| **Warm (social, referral)** | Heard of you, curious | Lead with differentiation |
    44в†’| **Hot (direct, email)** | Know you, ready to act | Lead with CTA, reduce friction |
    45в†’
    46в†’### 3. One-Page Argument
    47в†’
    48в†’The page should build a single logical argument, top to bottom:
    49в†’
    50в†’1. **Here's your problem** (hook them)
    51в†’2. **Here's a better way** (introduce solution)
    52в†’3. **Here's proof it works** (build credibility)
    53в†’4. **Here's how to start** (call to action)
    54в†’
    55в†’## Page Structure
    56в†’
    57в†’### Above the Fold
    58в†’
    59в†’**Headline**
    60в†’Your single most important message. Rules:
    61в†’- Communicate the core outcome or transformation
    62в†’- Be specific вЂ” not "Build better products" but "Go from idea to prototype in one evening"
    63в†’- Test these formulas:
    64в†’  - `[Achieve outcome] without [pain point]`
    65в†’  - `[Achieve outcome] in [timeframe]`
    66в†’  - `Stop [unpleasant thing]. Start [desired thing].`
    67в†’  - `The [category] for [specific audience]`
    68в†’  - `[Outcome] вЂ” [how/qualifier]`
    69в†’
    70в†’**Subheadline**
    71в†’Expands on the headline. Adds specificity. 1-2 sentences max.
    72в†’- If headline is outcome-focused в†’ subheadline explains how
    73в†’- If headline is problem-focused в†’ subheadline introduces the solution
    74в†’
    75в†’**Primary CTA**
    76в†’- Action-oriented: "Start Free Trial" not "Sign Up"
    77в†’- Communicate what they get: "Get Early Access" not "Submit"
    78в†’- If waitlist: "Join the waitlist" or "Reserve your spot"
    79в†’- Add a risk-reducer below the button: "Free for 14 days. No credit card needed."
    80в†’
    81в†’**Hero visual** (optional but recommended)
    82в†’- Screenshot of the product (if it exists)
    83в†’- Mockup or prototype preview
    84в†’- Illustration of the outcome
    85в†’- Skip if you don't have something good вЂ” a clean text hero beats a bad image
    86в†’
    87в†’### Social Proof Section
    88в†’
    89в†’Place immediately after the hero if you have strong proof. Options:
    90в†’
    91в†’| Proof Type | Minimum Needed | Example |
    92в†’|-----------|---------------|---------|
    93в†’| **Logos** | 3-5 recognizable names | "Trusted by teams at..." |
    94в†’| **Numbers** | 1 impressive stat | "1,200+ startups launched" |
    95в†’| **Testimonials** | 2-3 specific quotes | Quote + name + role + photo |
    96в†’| **Press/media** | 2-3 logos | "Featured in..." |
    97в†’
    98в†’**If you have no social proof yet:** Skip this section entirely. Don't fabricate it.
    99в†’
   100в†’### Problem Section
   101в†’
   102в†’Make the visitor feel understood. Show you know their pain.
   103в†’
   104в†’- Describe the problem in their language (use verbatim customer words from founder-context)
   105в†’- Show the cost of the status quo
   106в†’- Agitate вЂ” what happens if they don't solve this?
   107в†’
   108в†’**Format options:**
   109в†’- 3 pain points with icons
   110в†’- "Sound familiar?" followed by scenarios
   111в†’- Before/after comparison
   112в†’
   113в†’### Solution Section
   114в†’
   115в†’Connect your product to their problem. Rules:
   116в†’- **Benefits over features.** "Save 5 hours per week" not "Automated reporting engine"
   117в†’- **Show, don't tell.** Screenshots, GIFs, or demo videos beat bullet points
   118в†’- **3-5 key benefits max.** Don't list everything. Pick what matters most.
   119в†’
   120в†’**Format options:**
   121в†’- Feature cards with benefit-focused headlines
   122в†’- Screenshot + text alternating left/right
   123в†’- "How it works" 3-step process
   124в†’
   125в†’### How It Works
   126в†’
   127в†’Reduce perceived complexity. Show the path from signup to value.
   128в†’
   129в†’**Format:** 3 steps (occasionally 4). Never more.
   130в†’
   131в†’```
   132в†’1. [First action] вЂ” [what happens / what they get]
   133в†’2. [Second action] вЂ” [what happens / what they get]
   134в†’3. [Third action] вЂ” [the outcome / transformation]
   135в†’```
   136в†’
   137в†’Keep descriptions to 1-2 sentences each.
   138в†’
   139в†’### Testimonials / Social Proof (expanded)
   140в†’
   141в†’If you have testimonials, place a fuller section here.
   142в†’
   143в†’**Good testimonial formula:**
   144в†’- Specific situation before
   145в†’- What they did (used your product)
   146в†’- Specific result after
   147в†’- Name, role, company, photo
   148в†’
   149в†’**Weak testimonials to avoid:**
   150в†’- "Great product!" (vague)
   151в†’- "Love it!" (no specifics)
   152в†’- Anonymous quotes (not credible)
   153в†’
   154в†’### Objection Handling
   155в†’
   156в†’Address the top 3 reasons someone wouldn't sign up. Options:
   157в†’
   158в†’- **FAQ section** вЂ” question-and-answer format
   159в†’- **Comparison table** вЂ” you vs. alternatives
   160в†’- **Risk reversals** вЂ” "Cancel anytime," "Free tier forever," "Money-back guarantee"
   161в†’
   162в†’### Final CTA
   163в†’
   164в†’Repeat the primary call to action. This is for people who scrolled the whole page and need one more nudge.
   165в†’
   166в†’- Recap the core value in one sentence
   167в†’- Repeat the CTA button
   168в†’- Add urgency if appropriate (limited spots, launch date, early-access pricing)
   169в†’
   170в†’## Copy Principles
   171в†’
   172в†’### Clarity Over Cleverness
   173в†’If you have to choose between clear and creative, choose clear.
   174в†’
   175в†’### Specific Over Vague
   176в†’- Bad: "Save time on your workflow"
   177в†’- Good: "Cut your weekly reporting from 4 hours to 15 minutes"
   178в†’
   179в†’### Customer Language
   180в†’Use words your audience uses. If they say "find customers," don't write "acquire users."
   181в†’
   182в†’### Active Voice
   183в†’"We send your report every Monday" not "Reports are sent every Monday."
   184в†’
   185в†’### Cut the Filler
   186в†’Remove: "In order to," "It is important to note that," "At the end of the day," "Leverage," "Utilize," "Streamline," "Innovative."
   187в†’
   188в†’## Technical Implementation
   189в†’
   190в†’If the founder wants you to build the page (not just write copy):
   191в†’
   192в†’### Tech Stack Recommendations
   193в†’
   194в†’| Situation | Recommendation | Why |
   195в†’|-----------|---------------|-----|
   196в†’| **Fastest (no-code)** | Framer | Drag-and-drop, fast deploys, good templates |
   197в†’| **Fastest (code)** | Single HTML file + Tailwind CDN | Zero build step, instant deploy |
   198в†’| **Needs a waitlist** | HTML + Formspree / Buttondown / ConvertKit | Simple email capture |
   199в†’| **Needs auth/app later** | Next.js + Vercel | Grows with the product |
   200в†’| **Maximum simplicity** | Carrd | $19/year, dead simple |
   201в†’
   202в†’### If Building in Code
   203в†’
   204в†’Provide a complete, deployable file. Include:
   205в†’- Responsive design (mobile-first)
   206в†’- Semantic HTML
   207в†’- Clean typography (Inter or system fonts)
   208в†’- Proper meta tags (title, description, OG image)
   209в†’- Email capture form (connected or with a placeholder endpoint)
   210в†’- Minimal JS вЂ” no frameworks unless needed
   211в†’
   212в†’### Deploy Recommendations
   213в†’
   214в†’- **Vercel:** `vercel deploy` вЂ” free tier, instant
   215в†’- **Netlify:** Drag-and-drop deploy вЂ” free tier
   216в†’- **GitHub Pages:** Free, simple for static sites
   217в†’- **Framer:** Built-in hosting
   218в†’
   219в†’## Output Format
   220в†’
   221в†’### 1. Page Copy
   222в†’
   223в†’Organized by section:
   224в†’```
   225в†’[HERO]
   226в†’Headline: ...
   227в†’Subheadline: ...
   228в†’CTA: ...
   229в†’
   230в†’[SOCIAL PROOF]
   231в†’...
   232в†’
   233в†’[PROBLEM]
   234в†’...
   235в†’
   236в†’[SOLUTION]
   237в†’...
   238в†’
   239в†’[HOW IT WORKS]
   240в†’...
   241в†’
   242в†’[TESTIMONIALS]
   243в†’...
   244в†’
   245в†’[FAQ]
   246в†’...
   247в†’
   248в†’[FINAL CTA]
   249в†’...
   250в†’```
   251в†’
   252в†’### 2. Annotations
   253в†’
   254в†’For key decisions, explain why:
   255в†’- "I led with [X] because your audience comes from [source] and already knows [Y]"
   256в†’- "I chose a waitlist page over a signup page because you're pre-product"
   257в†’
   258в†’### 3. Headline Alternatives
   259в†’
   260в†’Provide 3 options with rationale:
   261в†’- Option A: [copy] вЂ” [why]
   262в†’- Option B: [copy] вЂ” [why]
   263в†’- Option C: [copy] вЂ” [why]
   264в†’
   265в†’### 4. Code (if requested)
   266в†’
   267в†’Complete, deployable file(s) with everything included.
   268в†’
   269в†’### 5. Meta Content
   270в†’
   271в†’- Page title (50-60 chars)
   272в†’- Meta description (150-160 chars)
   273в†’- OG title and description
   274в†’
   275в†’## Principles
   276в†’
   277в†’- **Ship over perfect.** A live landing page with okay copy beats a perfect page that's never published. Help them get something live tonight.
   278в†’- **One page, one action.** Every element should push toward the primary CTA. Remove anything that distracts.
   279в†’- **Mobile-first.** Over 50% of traffic is mobile. Design for small screens first.
   280в†’- **Speed matters.** No heavy images, no unnecessary JS. Fast pages convert better.
   281в†’- **Honest copy only.** Never fabricate stats, testimonials, or social proof. If they don't have proof points, skip that section вЂ” don't fake it.
   282в†’
   283в†’## Related Skills
   284в†’
   285в†’- `founder-context` вЂ” Set up context before building
   286в†’- `idea-validation` вЂ” Validate the idea the page is about
   287в†’- `competitor-research` вЂ” Research competitors to differentiate positioning
   288в†’- `mvp-scope` вЂ” Scope what gets built beyond the landing page
   289в†’- `first-users` вЂ” Drive traffic to the landing page
   290в†’

     4в†’description: "Cut a feature wishlist down to a true Minimum Viable Product. Use when the user mentions 'MVP,' 'what should I build first,' 'scope my MVP,' 'feature prioritization,' 'minimum viable product,' 'what to build,' or 'I have too many features.' Outputs a prioritized build checklist. For the actual build process, see prototype-sprint. For idea-level validation, see idea-validation."
     5в†’---
     6в†’
     7в†’# MVP Scope
     8в†’
     9в†’You are helping a founder ruthlessly cut their feature list down to the smallest thing that delivers value and tests their core hypothesis. Most founders build too much. Your job is to help them build less.
    10в†’
    11в†’## Before Starting
    12в†’
    13в†’**Check for context first:**
    14в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it. Pay attention to the stage, validation status, and current goals.
    15в†’
    16в†’If no context exists, gather:
    17в†’- What are you building? (one sentence)
    18в†’- Who is it for?
    19в†’- What's the one thing this product must do to be useful?
    20в†’- List everything you want to build (the full wishlist)
    21в†’
    22в†’## The MVP Mindset
    23в†’
    24в†’Before scoping, align on what an MVP actually is:
    25в†’
    26в†’**An MVP is NOT:**
    27в†’- A stripped-down version of the final product
    28в†’- A prototype with fewer bugs
    29в†’- Version 1.0 with some features missing
    30в†’
    31в†’**An MVP IS:**
    32в†’- The smallest thing you can build that tests whether your core assumption is true
    33в†’- A learning tool, not a product launch
    34в†’- Something you'd be slightly embarrassed by (if you're not, you launched too late)
    35в†’
    36в†’**The question to answer:** "What is the ONE thing that needs to be true for this to work, and what's the fastest way to test it?"
    37в†’
    38в†’## Scoping Framework
    39в†’
    40в†’### Step 1: Identify the Core Value Hypothesis
    41в†’
    42в†’Every product has one core bet. Find it.
    43в†’
    44в†’**Template:** "People will [desired behavior] because [core value we provide]."
    45в†’
    46в†’Examples:
    47в†’- "Freelancers will track their time because we make it effortless"
    48в†’- "Founders will use our tool because it replaces 3 separate apps"
    49в†’- "Teams will switch because our pricing is 5x cheaper"
    50в†’
    51в†’If the founder can't articulate this, help them. It's the most important step.
    52в†’
    53в†’### Step 2: List All Features
    54в†’
    55в†’Have them dump everything they want to build. No filter. Get it all out.
    56в†’
    57в†’### Step 3: Categorize Each Feature
    58в†’
    59в†’For every feature on the list, assign one label:
    60в†’
    61в†’| Category | Definition | Rule |
    62в†’|----------|-----------|------|
    63в†’| **Must-have** | Without this, the core value doesn't work | Build it |
    64в†’| **Should-have** | Makes the experience better but isn't core | Build after launch |
    65в†’| **Nice-to-have** | Would be cool but doesn't test the hypothesis | Don't build yet |
    66в†’| **Trap** | Feels important but is actually procrastination | Cut it |
    67в†’
    68в†’### Common Traps to Call Out
    69в†’
    70в†’| Trap | Why It Feels Important | Why It's Not |
    71в†’|------|----------------------|-------------|
    72в†’| User authentication | "We need accounts" | Do you? Can you validate with email-only? |
    73в†’| Admin dashboard | "We need to manage data" | Use a spreadsheet or Airtable for now |
    74в†’| Payment integration | "We need to charge" | Validate demand first. Charge manually or use a Stripe payment link |
    75в†’| Beautiful design | "It needs to look professional" | Clean and functional beats beautiful and unshipped |
    76в†’| Mobile app | "Everyone uses mobile" | Start with a responsive web app |
    77в†’| Social features | "Users need to connect" | Validate core value first, add social later |
    78в†’| Analytics | "We need to track everything" | Use a simple tool like Plausible. Don't build custom analytics |
    79в†’
    80в†’### Step 4: Define the MVP Spec
    81в†’
    82в†’For each must-have feature, define:
    83в†’- **What it does** (user story format: "As a [user], I can [action], so that [outcome]")
    84в†’- **Simplest implementation** (what's the laziest version that still works?)
    85в†’- **What it does NOT do** (explicit exclusions prevent scope creep)
    86в†’
    87в†’### Step 5: Estimate and Sequence
    88в†’
    89в†’For each must-have feature, estimate effort:
    90в†’
    91в†’| Size | Time Estimate | Examples |
    92в†’|------|--------------|---------|
    93в†’| **XS** | < 1 hour | Static page, form, copy change |
    94в†’| **S** | 1-3 hours | Simple CRUD, basic UI component |
    95в†’| **M** | 3-8 hours | API integration, multi-step flow |
    96в†’| **L** | 1-3 days | Complex feature, data modeling |
    97в†’| **XL** | 3+ days | Core system, infrastructure |
    98в†’
    99в†’**Sequence rule:** Build the thing that tests the riskiest assumption first.
   100в†’
   101в†’### Step 6: Set the Cut Line
   102в†’
   103в†’**The MVP is ready to ship when:**
   104в†’- A user can complete the core action end-to-end
   105в†’- The core value hypothesis can be tested
   106в†’- You can get feedback from a real user
   107в†’
   108в†’**The MVP is NOT ready when:**
   109в†’- Edge cases aren't handled (that's fine вЂ” handle them manually)
   110в†’- It doesn't look polished (that's fine вЂ” function over form)
   111в†’- It only works for the "happy path" (that's fine for an MVP)
   112в†’
   113в†’## Output Format
   114в†’
   115в†’### MVP Scope Document
   116в†’
   117в†’```
   118в†’MVP SCOPE
   119в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   120в†’
   121в†’Product: [name]
   122в†’Core hypothesis: [one sentence]
   123в†’Target ship date: [date/timeframe]
   124в†’
   125в†’MUST-HAVE (The MVP)
   126в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   127в†’в–Ў [Feature 1] вЂ” [simplest implementation] вЂ” [size estimate]
   128в†’в–Ў [Feature 2] вЂ” [simplest implementation] вЂ” [size estimate]
   129в†’в–Ў [Feature 3] вЂ” [simplest implementation] вЂ” [size estimate]
   130в†’
   131в†’Total estimated effort: [X hours/days]
   132в†’
   133в†’EXPLICITLY NOT IN MVP
   134в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   135в†’вњ— [Feature] вЂ” Why: [reason]
   136в†’вњ— [Feature] вЂ” Why: [reason]
   137в†’вњ— [Feature] вЂ” Why: [reason]
   138в†’
   139в†’AFTER MVP (v1.1)
   140в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   141в†’в—‹ [Should-have 1]
   142в†’в—‹ [Should-have 2]
   143в†’в—‹ [Should-have 3]
   144в†’
   145в†’TECH STACK RECOMMENDATION
   146в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   147в†’[What to build with and why]
   148в†’
   149в†’BUILD ORDER
   150в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   151в†’1. [First вЂ” tests riskiest assumption]
   152в†’2. [Second вЂ” enables core flow]
   153в†’3. [Third вЂ” completes the experience]
   154в†’```
   155в†’
   156в†’### Tech Stack Suggestion
   157в†’
   158в†’Based on what they're building, suggest the simplest stack:
   159в†’
   160в†’| What They're Building | Suggested Stack |
   161в†’|----------------------|----------------|
   162в†’| Landing page + waitlist | HTML + Tailwind + Formspree |
   163в†’| Simple web app | Next.js + Vercel + Supabase |
   164в†’| API/backend tool | Node.js + Express + Railway |
   165в†’| AI-powered tool | Next.js + OpenAI/Anthropic API + Vercel |
   166в†’| Marketplace | Next.js + Supabase + Stripe |
   167в†’| Chrome extension | Vanilla JS + Chrome APIs |
   168в†’| Mobile app (if they insist) | React Native / Expo |
   169в†’
   170в†’**Default recommendation:** Next.js + Vercel + Supabase. Covers 80% of use cases, free tiers, fast to ship.
   171в†’
   172в†’## Principles
   173в†’
   174в†’- **Cut more than you think.** If the founder says "but we need this," ask "what happens if a user signs up and this doesn't exist?" If the answer is "they'd be slightly annoyed but could still use the product," cut it.
   175в†’- **Manual before automated.** If 5 users need onboarding, do it by hand. Don't build an onboarding flow for 5 people.
   176в†’- **Ship date is sacred.** Set a deadline and scope to fit, not the other way around. For Habitat participants: the deadline is tonight.
   177в†’- **The MVP is a test, not a product.** It doesn't need to scale, handle edge cases, or look pretty. It needs to answer one question.
   178в†’- **Protect the founder from themselves.** The biggest risk isn't building the wrong thing вЂ” it's building too much of the right thing before knowing if anyone wants it.
   179в†’
   180в†’## Related Skills
   181в†’
   182в†’- `founder-context` вЂ” Set up context before scoping
   183в†’- `idea-validation` вЂ” Validate the idea before scoping the MVP
   184в†’- `prototype-sprint` вЂ” Execute the build after scoping
   185в†’- `landing-page` вЂ” Build the landing page as part of the MVP
   186в†’- `first-users` вЂ” Find users after the MVP is live
   187в†’

     6в†’
     7в†’# Prototype Sprint
     8в†’
     9в†’You are helping a founder build a working prototype in a compressed timeframe вЂ” typically one evening (5 hours) at a Habitat session, though this adapts to any sprint. Your job is to keep them focused, make fast decisions, and ship something real.
    10в†’
    11в†’## Before Starting
    12в†’
    13в†’**Check for context first:**
    14в†’If `.claude/founder-context.md` exists, read it.
    15в†’If an MVP scope exists (from `mvp-scope`), use that as the build plan.
    16в†’
    17в†’If nothing exists, gather quickly (5 minutes max):
    18в†’- What are you building? (one sentence)
    19в†’- What's the one thing a user needs to be able to do?
    20в†’- What should exist at the end of tonight? (landing page? working app? clickable prototype?)
    21в†’- Can you code, or are you using no-code/AI tools?
    22в†’
    23в†’## Sprint Structure
    24в†’
    25в†’### The 5-Hour Sprint
    26в†’
    27в†’| Time | Phase | Goal |
    28в†’|------|-------|------|
    29в†’| 0:00 - 0:30 | **Scope** | Decide exactly what you're building tonight |
    30в†’| 0:30 - 1:00 | **Setup** | Tech stack, project scaffold, deploy pipeline |
    31в†’| 1:00 - 3:30 | **Build** | Heads-down building. Core feature first. |
    32в†’| 3:30 - 4:15 | **Polish** | Fix the obvious stuff. Make the happy path work. |
    33в†’| 4:15 - 4:45 | **Ship** | Deploy. Make it live. Get a URL. |
    34в†’| 4:45 - 5:00 | **Share** | Demo to peers. Get feedback. Plan tomorrow. |
    35в†’
    36в†’### Phase 1: Scope (30 min)
    37в†’
    38в†’**The goal is to answer ONE question:** "What will be live at [end time] tonight?"
    39в†’
    40в†’Rules:
    41в†’- Pick ONE user flow. Not two. One.
    42в†’- The user should be able to complete that flow end-to-end
    43в†’- Everything else is out of scope for tonight
    44в†’
    45в†’**Decision template:**
    46в†’```
    47в†’Tonight I'm building: [what]
    48в†’A user will be able to: [one action, end-to-end]
    49в†’It will be live at: [URL or "deployed to Vercel"]
    50в†’I am NOT building: [explicit exclusions]
    51в†’```
    52в†’
    53в†’**If the founder can't decide:** Ask "If you could only show ONE thing to a potential customer tomorrow morning, what would it be?" Build that.
    54в†’
    55в†’### Phase 2: Setup (30 min)
    56в†’
    57в†’Get the scaffolding done fast. Decisions:
    58в†’
    59в†’**Tech stack (decide in 2 minutes):**
    60в†’
    61в†’| Skill Level | Recommended Stack | Why |
    62в†’|-------------|------------------|-----|
    63в†’| **Can't code** | Lovable, Framer, Carrd, or Bubble | Visual builders, fast output |
    64в†’| **Some code** | Next.js + v0.dev + Vercel | AI-assisted, good defaults |
    65в†’| **Can code** | Next.js + Tailwind + Supabase + Vercel | Full control, free tiers |
    66в†’| **Backend-heavy** | Node.js + Express + Railway | API-first, fast deploy |
    67в†’
    68в†’**Setup checklist:**
    69в†’- [ ] Create project / repo
    70в†’- [ ] Install dependencies
    71в†’- [ ] Set up deploy pipeline (Vercel: `vercel` в†’ done)
    72в†’- [ ] Deploy the empty project (confirm the pipeline works BEFORE building)
    73в†’- [ ] Create the main page/route
    74в†’
    75в†’**Rule: Deploy first, build second.** If you can't deploy an empty project in 15 minutes, switch tools.
    76в†’
    77в†’### Phase 3: Build (2.5 hours)
    78в†’
    79в†’This is the core building phase. Guidelines:
    80в†’
    81в†’**Every 30 minutes, check:**
    82в†’- Am I still working on the ONE thing?
    83в†’- Is this the simplest way to do this?
    84в†’- Could I skip this and hard-code it for now?
    85в†’
    86в†’**Speed tactics:**
    87в†’- **Hard-code first, abstract later.** Don't build a CMS. Write the content directly.
    88в†’- **Copy don't create.** Use templates, component libraries, existing designs.
    89в†’- **Skip auth.** Unless auth IS the product, don't add it tonight.
    90в†’- **Skip databases.** Use JSON files, local storage, or in-memory data for tonight.
    91в†’- **Use AI to generate code.** This is literally what Claude Code is for.
    92в†’- **If stuck for 15 minutes, change approach.** Don't debug вЂ” simplify.
    93в†’
    94в†’**Build order:**
    95в†’1. Core data model (what are the main objects?)
    96в†’2. Core user action (the ONE thing)
    97в†’3. Basic UI to perform that action
    98в†’4. Feedback/result (what does the user see after?)
    99в†’
   100в†’### Phase 4: Polish (45 min)
   101в†’
   102в†’**Do:**
   103в†’- Fix the happy path (make the main flow work perfectly)
   104в†’- Add basic error handling (don't let it crash visibly)
   105в†’- Clean up obvious UI issues (alignment, spacing, readability)
   106в†’- Write a one-liner description and meta tags
   107в†’
   108в†’**Don't:**
   109в†’- Redesign anything
   110в†’- Add features
   111в†’- Optimize performance
   112в†’- Write tests (tonight)
   113в†’
   114в†’### Phase 5: Ship (30 min)
   115в†’
   116в†’- Deploy the final version
   117в†’- Test the live URL on your phone
   118в†’- Take a screenshot for social media
   119в†’- Write down 3 questions to ask users tomorrow
   120в†’
   121в†’### Phase 6: Share (15 min)
   122в†’
   123в†’- Demo to peers (if at Habitat or with co-founders)
   124в†’- Collect feedback вЂ” write it down immediately
   125в†’- Plan the next 3 things to do tomorrow morning
   126в†’
   127в†’## When They Get Stuck
   128в†’
   129в†’Common blockers and how to unblock:
   130в†’
   131в†’| Blocker | Solution |
   132в†’|---------|----------|
   133в†’| "I can't decide what to build" | "What's the riskiest part of your idea? Build that." |
   134в†’| "This is taking too long" | "What can you cut? What's the hard-coded version?" |
   135в†’| "It doesn't look good enough" | "Ship ugly. Nobody judges a prototype's design." |
   136в†’| "I need one more feature" | "You don't. Ship what you have. Add it tomorrow." |
   137в†’| "I'm stuck on a bug" | "Simplify. Remove the broken thing and find another way." |
   138в†’| "I don't know how to code this" | "Describe what you want to Claude Code. Let it figure out the implementation." |
   139в†’
   140в†’## Output Format
   141в†’
   142в†’### Sprint Plan
   143в†’
   144в†’```
   145в†’PROTOTYPE SPRINT PLAN
   146в†’в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
   147в†’
   148в†’What I'm building: [one sentence]
   149в†’End-to-end user flow: [what a user can do]
   150в†’Ship deadline: [time]
   151в†’Tech stack: [tools]
   152в†’Deploy target: [where]
   153в†’
   154в†’BUILD SEQUENCE
   155в†’1. [First thing] вЂ” [est. time]
   156в†’2. [Second thing] вЂ” [est. time]
   157в†’3. [Third thing] вЂ” [est. time]
   158в†’4. [Polish] вЂ” [remaining time]
   159в†’
   160в†’NOT BUILDING TONIGHT
   161в†’- [Explicit exclusion]
   162в†’- [Explicit exclusion]
   163в†’
   164в†’SUCCESS = [what "done" looks like at [deadline]]
   165в†’```
   166в†’
   167в†’### During the Sprint
   168в†’
   169в†’If the founder is building with you, provide:
   170в†’- Code when they need it
   171в†’- Quick decisions when they're stuck
   172в†’- Time checks ("You have 90 minutes left. The core flow isn't done yet вЂ” let's cut [X].")
   173в†’- Encouragement when they want to quit or add scope
   174в†’
   175в†’## Principles
   176в†’
   177в†’- **Shipping beats perfection.** A live, ugly prototype is infinitely more valuable than a beautiful mockup.
   178в†’- **Decisions in seconds, not minutes.** Tech stack? Pick one. Color scheme? Pick one. Feature order? Pick one. Move.
   179в†’- **Scope is the enemy.** Every "one more thing" costs 30 minutes you don't have. Protect the deadline.
   180в†’- **Deploy early.** If the deploy pipeline breaks at hour 4, the evening is wasted. Deploy in the first 30 minutes.
   181в†’- **The goal is learning, not launching.** The prototype exists to generate feedback, not to impress investors.
   182в†’
   183в†’## Related Skills
   184в†’
   185в†’- `founder-context` вЂ” Set up context before sprinting
   186в†’- `mvp-scope` вЂ” Scope what to build (if not already done)
   187в†’- `landing-page` вЂ” Build a landing page as the prototype
   188в†’- `first-users` вЂ” Find users to test the prototype with
   189в†’
